content:
Reinforcement learning grew from two threads: **optimal control** (Bellman's dynamic programming in the 1950s) and **trial-and-error learning** in psychology and animal behavior experiments.

Richard **Bellman** formalized the **Bellman equation** and value iteration, showing how optimal policies satisfy recursive relationships between states. At the same time, researchers studied how animals learn from reward and punishment—ideas later formalized as **reinforcement** in learning theory.

**Minsky** and others explored learning machines in the 1950s–60s; **Watkins**'s **Q-learning** (1989) gave a simple, model-free way for agents to learn action values from experience. **Sutton** and **Barto** unified the field in *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction*, emphasizing temporal-difference learning and the agent–environment interface.

**Deep Q-Networks (DQN, 2015)** combined Q-learning with deep neural networks, achieving human-level play on many Atari games and sparking modern **deep RL**. Robotics, game AI, and recommender systems adopted RL at scale.

In the 2020s, **RLHF** brought RL vocabulary back to the center of generative AI: language models as policies, human preferences as rewards—connecting classical RL to alignment of large models.

timeline.label: 1950s — Dynamic programming
timeline.description: Bellman equations frame sequential decision problems; optimal control theory precedes modern RL algorithms.

timeline.label: 1989 — Q-learning
timeline.description: Watkins proves convergence properties for model-free learning of action values from experience.

timeline.label: 1998 — Sutton & Barto textbook
timeline.description: Standard reference codifies MDPs, temporal-difference methods, and policy improvement.

timeline.label: 2015 — Deep RL breakthroughs
timeline.description: DQN and policy-gradient methods scale RL to high-dimensional perception (games, simulators).

timeline.label: 2020s — RLHF for LLMs
timeline.description: Preference rewards align generative models, reusing agent–policy–reward framing at language scale.
